data.title: Silver Rally Lifts Miner Stocks With Expectations Of Strong Revenue Growth
data.description:
Silver has staged a scintillating rally, climbing to near record highs as investors see precious metals as safer assets amid persistent economic and geopolitical uncertainty. Silver prices have risen sharply over the past year alongside gold, buoyed by expectations of easier monetary policy and a weaker dollar. But silver’s story runs deeper than macro hedging. Industrial demand, particularly from solar panels, power electronics and electrification, continues to accelerate, exacerbating a structural supply deficit and adding fuel to the price momentum.

This price surge has also translated into extraordinary equity gains for listed silver producers. Among some of the largest silver-focused miners tracked by Visible Alpha, UK-based Fresnillo (LSE:FRES)(FNLPF) and North American peers Pan American Silver (PAAS), Coeur Mining (CDE) and First Majestic Silver (AG) have seen their share prices soar by 583%, 166%, 260% and 296% year-on-year, respectively.

Visible Alpha consensus suggests the earnings tailwind is also beginning to show up in analyst forecasts. Fresnillo, the world’s largest primary silver producer, is expected to see a 23% year-on-year revenue growth in 2025 to $4.3 billion, according to Visible Alpha estimates. Silver, which accounts for about 46% of the group’s total revenue, is projected to generate roughly $2 billion in sales, up 19% from the prior year.

North American producers show an even sharper rebound in silver-specific revenues. Pan American Silver is forecast to see silver revenues jump 63% year-on-year to $891 million in 2025. Coeur Mining and First Majestic Silver are expected to post increases of 125% and 163%, lifting silver revenues to $717 million and $634 million, respectively.

A closer look at price and volume dynamics reveals that much of this revenue growth is price-led, though the mix varies by producer. Realized silver prices are forecast to see a sharp increase across the peer group compared with recent trough levels, reflecting the powerful upswing in spot prices.

For Fresnillo, the uplift is almost entirely driven by pricing. Silver volumes sold are expected to fall 13% year-on-year from 57.2 million troy ounces in 2024 to 49.9 million troy ounces in 2025. Even so, silver revenue is projected to rise as realized prices climb 37% year-on-year, from $29.1 per troy ounce to about $40.

North American producers offer a more balanced picture. Pan American Silver is expected to benefit from both higher prices and modest output growth, with realized prices rising 39% year-on-year to $39.1 per troy ounce in 2025, alongside a 17% increase in volumes to 22.8 million troy ounces.

Coeur Mining and First Majestic Silver exhibit the greatest operational leverage to the rally, with production growth playing an outsized role. Coeur’s realized prices are projected to rise 37% year-on-year to $38.3 per troy ounce in 2025, while volumes are forecast to jump 64% to 18.7 million troy ounces. First Majestic is expected to see realized prices increase 38% to about $39 per troy ounce, with volumes surging 86% to 15.9 million troy ounces.

At current levels, the key risk is sustainability. Silver prices remain inherently volatile and highly sensitive to shifts in monetary policy expectations. While the recent rally has materially boosted earnings, a moderation in prices could quickly temper revenue growth.

data.title: Manning & Napier Annual Outlook 2026
data.description:
By now, most investors understand that markets do not move in straight lines. What feels different heading into 2026 is not uncertainty itself, but the number of forces intersecting at once. Economic signals are mixed. Policy changes are reshaping tax decisions.

Major stock indexes around the world produced double-digit returns for the year. For individuals and families, the question is no longer how to react, but how to plan through it with clarity and purpose.

At moments like this, the value of a comprehensive financial plan becomes especially clear. Investing matters, but it is only one aspect of a much larger strategy that must evolve alongside your life and the broader financial landscape.

This Environment Demands More

Over the past several years, strong equity market performance has carried many portfolios forward. That success, while welcome, can sometimes obscure underlying risks. Concentration in a narrow group of market leaders, uneven exposure across accounts, and outdated assumptions about taxes or retirement timelines often go unnoticed during favorable markets.

As we look ahead to 2026, planning conversations are shifting. The questions are nuanced: Are financial plans resilient? Are there opportunities to make the most of today’s tax rules before or as they change? Do portfolios still reflect long-term goals, not just driven by recent performance?

These are the right questions. They reflect a broader truth that wealth is not static, and neither is the environment surrounding it. And the exact aspects our financial consultants are addressing in client discussions. Now, more than ever, it’s a balance between financial planning and an active investment strategy.

Retirement Is No Longer a Finish Line

One of the most important planning realities shaping 2026 is longevity. Retirement today often spans two or even three decades. That extended horizon places greater emphasis on sustainable savings.

Contribution limits for retirement accounts have increased for the year, creating meaningful opportunities for those still working to grow their nest-egg.

For many households, especially those in peak earning years, maximizing these vehicles is about preserving optionality in the future. Thoughtful coordination between taxable, tax-deferred, and tax-free accounts can meaningfully influence how retirement income is generated years from now.

Retirement planning is no longer a question of when you stop working. It is a question of how you maintain control and confidence across an extended and evolving phase of life.

Taxes Are a Planning Variable, Not a Once-a-Year Task

With the introduction of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act in summer 2025, the tax landscape has shifted, and the changes have introduced both complexity and opportunity. New rules affect deductions, charitable strategies, and how different forms of income are treated. For high-net-worth families, these changes reinforce the importance of proactive planning rather than reactive filing.

Capital gains, in particular, deserve careful attention. The difference between long-term and short-term treatment, as well as the timing of gains across years, can materially affect after-tax outcomes. Incremental realization, strategic loss harvesting, and coordination with broader income streams, all play a role in managing taxes within your plan.

Thinking About Portfolios in 2026

The new year will bring its own surprises, opportunities, and challenges for capital markets. For us, at the outset of 2026, several important questions stand out:

  • Will artificial intelligence yield tangible, economy-wide benefits quickly enough for companies and investors to justify the ongoing boom in AI spending?
  • Can pro-business policies, such as tax incentives and deregulation, lead to stronger economic activity by encouraging increased investment and hiring?
  • Is the labor market experiencing a temporary soft patch, or is a more sustained downturn on the horizon?

Maintaining a diversified approach to portfolio construction is as important as ever. Diversification enables investors to be positioned to benefit from favorable outcomes to these key questions, while also helping to mitigate the risks associated with excessive exposure to adverse developments.

History reminds us that no asset class wins every month, quarter, year – or every 5 years.

The Power of Diversification

5-Year Annualized Total Returns

Analysis: Manning & Napier. Source: Bloomberg (01/01/2001 – 12/31/2025).

Looking Ahead

As we enter 2026, perhaps the most important takeaway is that planning is not a one-time event. It is an ongoing dialogue. Life changes. Markets change. Laws change. A plan that adapts alongside those shifts is far more valuable than one that sits untouched.

Regular check-ins allow for proactive adjustments before they become urgent. They create space to revisit assumptions, refine strategies, and ensure that each decision fits within your broader goals. In a world that feels increasingly complex, that alignment is often what clients value most.

No outlook can predict the future with certainty. What it can do is provide context, perspective, and a framework for decision-making. As we move into 2026, the environment calls for intention over reaction and planning over prediction, so this is a moment to step back, take stock, and ensure that your financial strategy reflects not just where markets have been, but where your life is headed.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Polymarket For The Polycrisis
data.description:
Imagine if they had Polymarket (POLYMARKET) and Kalshi (KALSHI) 150 years ago. What a time the speculators of the day would have had! In 1876, major disturbances broke out all over the Balkans as people rose up, in a generally sporadic and uncoordinated fashion, against their nominal overlords running the Ottoman Empire.

An uprising in Bulgaria was particularly brutal, as a small band of nationalists was crushed by Ottoman troops and massacred in grisly fashion. These disturbances worried the Great Powers of the day, with Russia and newly-united Germany in particular swooping in to either try and restore order or grab a bit of land and influence where the opportunity presented itself. Serbia and Bosnia were also waking up to nationalist sentiments that would reach a fever pitch in the years to come.

Prediction Markets Mania

Of course, there was no such thing as digital prediction market platforms back in the 1870s, so there was no way for, say a Vienna-based punter to make an overnight killing on the likelihood that the Ottomans would accept the establishment of an independent Bulgaria, or the odds of a Bosnian revolt against the administration of their affairs by Austria-Hungary. Today, though, these user-friendly market platforms are in full swing as the Western world reverts to the mores of Great Power politics.

And what happens on prediction platforms like Polymarket doesn’t stay on Polymarket, but jumps like a charged electron up a level or two to exert pressure on flesh-and-blood securities indexes like the S&P 500.

That index was all over the place on Tuesday and Wednesday as investors placed their bets on the likelihood of a Greenland invasion, or the follow-through with another round of punishing tariffs on Europe.

When, in the middle of a speech on Wednesday, the three-word chain “won’t use force” manifested, the reaction on the stock indexes was immediate, and those who had placed “no invasion” bets on Polymarket cashed in their chips. For a textbook illustration of how financial assets move in the world of today, one could do worse than to study the minutiae of Tuesday and Wednesday this week.

Lots of Events, Absence of Vision

Polymarket, Kalshi and their ilk are called “event contract” platforms, a bit of technicalese meant to distinguish them from (heaven forbid) gambling sites. They are fast becoming a central part of the mainstream financial market infrastructure.

Fortunately for their executives and shareholders, there are plenty of events to wager on as this new geopolitical era dawns. Few people imagine that Trump’s speech on Wednesday marked the last time Greenland will be in the news, but never fear, there will be other “events” in the weeks ahead.

And because the data around these bets (sorry, “event contracts”) tend to get instant visibility in the financial media, they have the ability to validate an imagined future outcome before the nuts and bolts behind that event have been deliberated on by those involved in and responsible for negotiations around it.

We run the risk of pinballing from one crisis to the next without any kind of through-line to connect the dots. This adds a whole new dimension of risk to what observers like Adam Tooze, the Columbia professor and author of the widely read Substack “Chartbook” call the “polycrisis,” the simultaneous presence of crises on multiple fronts, about multiple things.

This lack of vision brings back to mind those unsettled days in the Balkans in the late 1870s. The tumult seemed to die down in 1878 with the Treaty of Berlin, an ambitious attempt to keep the Ottoman Empire from completely collapsing while at the same time giving nationalists in Serbia, Bulgaria and elsewhere a bit of opportunity to express themselves but within the strictures of administration and guidance from the Great Powers.

In a practical sense – though only a handful of observers realized it at the time – the Treaty of Berlin set two of these Great Powers – Tsarist Russia and the Hapsburg Empire of Austria-Hungary – implacably against each other. Germany, worried about Russia’s encouragement of pan-Slavism in the Balkans, bound itself to Austria-Hungary and then with Italy in a Triple Alliance. The bonds between Russia and France tightened at the same time and would eventually lead to the Entente Cordiale between these two countries and Great Britain.

The Concert of Europe - a global order that had managed, more or less, to keep the peace since the end of the Napoleonic Wars - was thus replaced after the Treaty of Berlin by the formation of two great-power alliances that became ever more belligerent and defensive as Europe reeled from one crisis to the next, in the Balkans, in Algeria and elsewhere, before it all blew up in 1914.

Such are the risks of the great power mentality that we appear to have stumbled back into. We are going to be in need of clear, strategic thinking as we move towards whatever the new world order is going to be – and we would be well advised to pay little heed to what the Polymarket “odds” that seem to accompany every news story of geopolitical import tell us.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Annexon Offers A High-Risk, High-Reward Play On A New Approach To Complement-Mediated Diseases
data.description:
Although systemic complement-mediated diseases are, fortunately, relatively rare, there is a wider range of diseases where complement overactivation plays a significant role, and these are often hard-to-treat diseases with serious quality of life implications. Annexon (ANNX) is looking to change this with a pipeline of risky, but high-potential, drugs targeting the C1q protein in a range of indications.

Tanruprubart could be the company’s first commercialized drug, targeting Guillain-Barre Syndrome (or GBS), while Phase III vonaprument could generate considerably greater revenue in geographic atrophy. Further back in the pipeline, ANX1502 isn’t particularly interesting for its primary lead indication today (cold agglutinin disease), but it’s an oral compound, and proof of concept here could open up an attractive list of addressable conditions for an easier administration pathway.

I want to emphasize that Annexon is a risky proposition, even by the standards of biotech, and there are multiple clinical and regulatory hurdles between here and success. That said, the risk-reward looks interesting today, and 2026 could see several meaningful positive milestones.

Tanruprubart – Greater Clarity On The U.S. Path To Market Is Needed

Annexon’s most advanced product candidate is tanruprubart, an intravenous antibody that targets the C1q protein and has completed Phase III testing for use in Guillain-Barre Syndrome.

GBS is fortunately rare, affecting about 7,000 people in the U.S. and roughly twice that in the EU. An acute autoimmune disorder, GBS sees the patient’s immune system attack its own peripheral nerves (often after an otherwise common and unremarkable infection), leading to rapid-onset muscle weakness that can advance to paralysis and respiratory failure. While most patients eventually recover, it can take years to do so (and some patients die or suffer prolonged disability), and there are limited therapeutic options, as intravenous immunoglobulin and/or plasmapheresis help some, but not all, patients.

A Successful Study … With Asterisks

While Annexon has completed a successful Phase III study, it is not without controversy and risk. The study was run outside of the U.S., and my understanding is that this was because IVIG and plasmapheresis are not specifically approved therapies, but running a placebo-controlled study when they’re available would create ethical issues for U.S. sites.

Adding to the controversy, while the 30mg/kg dose achieved a statistically significant outcome on the primary endpoint (change in GBS Disability Scale, or GBS-DS) and multiple secondary endpoints, the 75mg/kg dose did not. While there were directionally positive results for 75mg/kg, management has theorized that over-inhibition of C1q could ultimately be counterproductive for tissue repair—it's a plausible theory, but regulators and analysts are understandably nervous when studies don’t show dose-dependent responses.

As far as those results go, in the 30mg/kg group, 71% of patients were categorized as a “4” (bedridden or chairbound) at the trial start, with the remainder in category 3 (15%) and category 5 (14%), while the placebo group was 9% in 3, 79% in 4, and 12% in 5. After 8 weeks of treatment, 29% of the 30mg/kg group were classified as 0 or 1 (versus 12% in placebo), 44% as 2 or 3 (51% in placebo), and 27% in 4 to 6 (37% in placebo). Said differently, after Week 8, 73% of patients were wholly or partially mobile versus 63% in the placebo group and nearly 85% were immobile at trial start.

For the 75mg/kg dose, about 88% of patients started at 4 or 5, and after 8 weeks of treatment, 67% were at least partially mobile (a score of 0 to 3) versus 63% in the placebo group.

Looking at other endpoints, MRC sum score (a measure of muscle strength) was 10 points better than placebo at Day 8 and 4 points better at Week 8, and this is a good indicator of future disability outcomes. While the decline over time may seem worrisome, this is typical in GBS studies, as many patients naturally start to improve over time even on placebo. Half as many 30mg/kg patients needed ventilation, and there were 28 fewer days of ventilation (median) in the 30mg/kg group.

Safety was clean, with similar Grade 3+ TAE rates across the 30mg/kg group (42%), the 75mg/kg group (44%), and the placebo group (43%).

Still Looking For Clarity On The Path Forward In The U.S.

Annexon has already filed for approval of tanruprubart in the EU and is looking for potential commercial partners to market the drug outside the United States.

The U.S. path is less clear at this point. As of the company’s last update, they have filed for another Type C meeting with the FDA to discuss the process. The main hangup at the FDA seems to be how generalizable the study results are to U.S. patients. The study was conducted in the Philippines and Bangladesh, and there are regional differences in disease variants and clinical features, not to mention patient differences as well – Asian patients tend to have lower muscle mass, for instance, and GBS in Asia tends to present with more pure motor disease and less sensorimotor disease versus the U.S./EU.

To help settle this question, the company launched the open-label FORWARD study that will enroll up to 30 Western patients (at least two-thirds in the U.S.). Assuming that the FORWARD study delivers comparable results, management could file a BLA with this data to support and augment the Phase III study results.

It remains to be seen how the FDA decides to proceed. The FDA could allow Annexon to file as is, file with the inclusion of data from the FORWARD study (assuming it is successful / supportive) or insist on a controlled study in the United States. At this point I’m assuming that Annexon can apply for approval for tanruprubart without another larger study, but will need the FORWARD results, and I think there’s a better than 50/50 chance of eventual approval.

Vonaprument Is Promising, But Has Its Own “Buts”…

Annexon is also in advanced clinical development with vonaprument, an antigen-binding fragment that targets C1q in geographic atrophy.

Geographic atrophy is an advanced form of dry AMD and it leads to the gradual and irreversible loss of retinal cells, which in turn leads to permanent blind spots (with those “spots” tending to grow over time). While largely an age-related disease, overactivation of complement proteins drives the chronic inflammation and cell death that characterizes the disease.

Clinical studies to date suggest that blocking C1q has neuroprotective value, slowing inflammation, neuronal damage, and synapse loss. While this approach dose not seem to impact the growth of GA lesions in the eye, it does appear to have a meaningful impact on preserving vision, setting it apart from other approved GA drugs like Apellis’s (APLS) Syfovre (a C3 inhibitor) and Astellas’s (ALPMY) Izervay (a C5 inhibitor).

This is the main “but” about this potential drug – until now, efficacy for GA drugs has been measured by slowing the growth of GA lesions, and vonaprument doesn’t seem to do much there (a 6% reduction in lesion area change from baseline, with some greater efficacy over time).

Looking at vision preservation, though, as measured by BCVA ≥ 15-letter loss through 12 months, every-month dosing showed a 5.6% loss in Phase II testing versus 9.8% in every-other-month dosing and 21.3% in the sham group. Results were similar in low-luminance visual acuity (or LLVA) with 7.6% LLVA ≥ 15-letter loss in the every-month group versus 20.3% in the sham group. With monthly dosing, the Phase II study showed a 73% reduction in risk of vision loss through 12 months, and there was a significant difference when patients went off treatment.

Syvfovre hasn’t shown significant BCVA ≥15-letter loss benefit, though longer-term follow-up has shown some benefit with severe impairment (a greater than 35-letter loss), and while Izervay has shown a modest post-hoc benefit for ≥15-letter loss (4% versus 7.6% in the sham group) in a pooled analysis of the GATHER1 and GATHER2 studies, two-year data from GATHER2 saw no difference in ≥15-letter persistent vision loss.

Not surprisingly, then, Annexon has designed its Phase III ARCHER II study with the percentage of patients with BCVA ≥15-letter loss as the primary endpoint. The study completed enrollment two months earlier than planned and enrolled more patients than expected, and the study design was more stringent than Phase II in terms of excluding patients with more advanced vision loss (BCVA enrichment of ≥45 in Phase III versus ≥24 letters in Phase II).

I believe this focus on vision preservation will resonate with patients and clinicians, but there’s always a risk when you present different endpoints to the FDA than what they’re used to (even when the agency signs off on the trial design). I believe this study is likely to show a meaningful benefit, and I think it could position the drug as an earlier-stage alternative for patients with less severe disease, particularly as it seems to work better in patients with less severe disease.

While there are an estimated 4M people with GA in the U.S. and EU, many don’t seek treatment. Even so, if the addressable market is only about one-third of the patient population, that’s enough to drive a meaningful addressable market. Another issue to consider is competition – not only are Syfovre and Izervay on the market, there are over a half-dozen compounds in Phase III or Phase II development, including compounds from Regeneron (REGN), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Boeringher Ingelheim, and Galimedix.

Oral C1 Treatment Is A Call Option

Further behind is ANX1502, the company’s oral C1 program. While management is initially targeted cold agglutinin disease in its Phase II proof of concept study, demonstrating clinical efficacy with an oral compound could potentially open up an attractive array of future target indications that could welcome more convenient and comfortable drug administration (versus IV and subcutaneous administration).

Cold agglutinin disease is rare (likely around 2,000 to 3,000 patients in the U.S.) and not likely to support a large addressable revenue opportunity (maybe $500M to $600M at most), but a path to oral complement drugs could have meaningful long-term value.

The Outlook

Annexon ended the last quarter with $189M in cash and then raised another $86M through a share and warrant offering. With the company burning around $50M/quarter in cash (year-to-date run-rate through Q3), this should be enough to get the company well into 2027 and through to an FDA decision on the filing path for tanruprubart, Phase III results for vonaprument, and potential commercial partnership in Europe for tanruprubart. Moreover, a commercial partnership could bring in cash, and positive developments (with the FDA and/or clinic) would likely boost the stock and allow for further fundraising on less dilutive terms.

I believe tanruprubart could become a $2B drug even with a relatively small patient population given the lack of treatment alternatives and the cost of IVIG and plasmapheresis (not to mention supportive care). Tanruprubart is worth more than $4/share in my model today, but clarity on the U.S. approval pathway could quickly add around $1/share through derisking.

I believe vonaprument could be an even larger drug for Annexon, with 20% to 25% share in the U.S. and EU GA markets potentially worth multiple billions of dollars. With a roughly two-thirds chance of approval (consistent with typical Phase III programs), this drug alone is worth about $10/share.

The value of ANX1502 is far more speculative at this point, but each $1B of addressable revenue from oral complement inhibitors would add about $0.50/share in value today and I believe management could progress relatively quickly into studies after positive proof of concept once the funding situation is more stable.

The Bottom Line

All told, I believe Annexon is worth around $14.50/share and that’s with what I believe to be relatively conservative (or at least reasonable) risk weightings on the company’s lead programs. This year (2026) is going to be a big one for the company, with clarity on FDA approval and the efficacy of vonaprument either driving meaningful value realization or snuffing out most of the potential. It’s a high-risk high-reward proposition, but one that I think is worth a look from investors who can accept the elevated risks of pre-commercial biotech.

data.title: Trex: Soft Demand And Limited Upside In A Stubborn Repair And Remodel Downturn
data.description:
Introduction

Trex Company (TREX) is an established player in the composite decking and railing market. Over the last year, shares have lost about a third of their value as the repair and remodeling space has been under pressure. After a surge during the pandemic when homeowners poured money into home improvements, activity has slowed dramatically. Higher interest rates, lingering economic uncertainty, and a pull-forward of demand have left the sector under pressure, and Trex has not been immune at all. While the company continues to benefit from its strong brand and cost discipline, softer consumer spending and cautious inventory management by channel partners have made results more volatile than many anticipated.

In this article, I’ll walk through Trex's business performance amid the industry headwinds, why the repair and remodel slowdown is proving more stubborn than expected, and how I'm thinking about the competitive landscape and margin headwinds ahead. Finally, I’ll discuss why the valuation leaves room for little upside from today's levels.

Trex’s Q3 results at a glance

Trex came in with weaker than expected Q3 results, with a miss on both the top and bottom lines. During the quarter, the company’s net sales came in at $285.4 million, missing analyst estimates by $16.4 million despite an increase of 22.1% year over year. Across the product portfolio, the company saw strength, especially in railing, and the absence of the channel inventory destocking pressure that weighed on the comparable period helped the bottom line too.

Moving down the income statement, one thing I think is important to call out is on margins. While most of the industry is experiencing gross margin compression in Q3, Trex actually saw a margin increase of 60 bps to 40.5%. With gross profit dollars climbing to $115 million, up 23.9% compared to last year, Trex saw lower labor costs had realized efficiencies through its cost improvement programs. That said, on year-to-date figures, gross profit is down 4.6%, and gross margins were down 320 bps.

On the SG&A side, Trex had selling and general expenses at 15.8% of revenues compared to 16.6% last year, and thanks to higher investments in branding and IT, the company’s bottom line performance has improved materially. As such, EPS of $0.51 was up 28% from last year, but that still represented a miss to the sell-side's expectations for 57 cents (source: Bloomberg).

Setting up for a weaker 2026

While business has held up okay, Trex did report a miss, and guidance for 2026 was a bit weak too. After the results were released, the stock fell from $48 to $32 the next morning, indicating that the market didn’t like what it saw.

One of the big reasons for this (beyond the miss) was that the company reduced its full year guidance. Now, management expects sales to be flat versus up 5-7%, and adjusted EBITDA is now expected to be 28.0-28.5% vs. 31%+ previously.

The core issue was that Trex's Q3 results fell short of expectations, with net sales of $285 million coming in 5% below the midpoint of their own guidance and leading to a perception of weakening demand in the repair and remodel sector. I think this was also somewhat compounded by a sequential revenue drop of about 26% the quarter prior.

In my view, investors were particularly rattled because just three months earlier, in August, management had touted their level-loading inventory strategy as a way to reduce volatility and stabilize results, yet the Q3 miss and muted Q4 outlook. Management cited ongoing consumer demand softness after July and channel partners deliberately lowering inventories. That seemed to suggest that that approach wasn't delivering as promised, creating doubts about execution and near-term visibility.

So where does the company go from here?

It’s no secret that the overall repair and remodel (R&R) space has been under pressure. Post-COVID, there’s been four years of muted activity as a lot of demand got pulled forward. During the pandemic, people went out and spent money on fixing their decks and buying railings for them. After all, people had extra money and were staying home more.

Today, the picture looks a lot different. Right now, there have been a lot of prolonged R&R headwinds that stem from higher interest rates and more competitive pressures over the company's ability to convert wood-to-composite demand into actual growth amid economic uncertainty. For next quarter, management is guiding for $140-150 million in sales which really speaks to the softness in consumer demand and channel partners reducing their inventories. At some point, that weakness will end, but until new housing starts pick back up or we see uplift in the overall R&R space, we probably won't see a recovery on the top line until the summer months, in my view.

What’s perhaps more troubling is that I don’t see an easy way for Trex to grow their margins much. Assuming a 26% EBITDA margin for FY’26, accounting for headwinds guided by management, that’s not much below the company’s average EBITDA margin of 27.6% that it had during 2015-2019, pre-COVID. On the earnings call, management noted certain discrete FY'26 headwinds, including SG&A rising to 18% of sales (vs. 16.5% in FY’25), along with rail mix & Little Rock depreciation driving -250 bps of gross margin pressure. And while James Hardie’s (JHX) acquisition of AZEK has intensified near-term competition, it’s not clear to me where investors ought to expect some normalization even if the demand environment improves.

On the positive side, I think Trex has a flexible balance sheet that doesn’t have much leverage. With a small line of credit of $111 million against EBITDA of 342 million, the company maintains adequate flexibility should it want to take on debt to fund expansion, acquisitions, or return cash to shareholders via dividends and share buybacks.

Minimal upside without a turn in the housing market

In thinking through the risks of investing in Trex, I think a lot depends on the prolonged weakness we are seeing in R&R activity and the fact that the business is heavily reliant on discretionary consumer demand. Shown below, the latest Leading Indicator of Remodeling Activity (LIRA) from Harvard's Joint Center for Housing Studies shows that spending on improvements and repairs to owner-occupied homes should stay fairly steady into this year. Growth is expected to pick up a bit in early 2026 before slowing slightly later in the year (modest improvement). After a big surge a few years ago followed by a slowdown (even some declines in 2023–2024), spending has started to edge back up this year. Overall, total remodeling spending is on track to hit a new record high next year, and some improvement in demand is likely supported by more remodeling permits and steady single-family home sales.

Where the risk comes in is if slower new home construction and general uncertainty around the economy keep things from growing faster. As shown below, building permits in October were 0.2% below the revised rate in September, and housing starts were 4.6% below the revised September estimate. With still-high interest rates and a weaker job market, unless the housing market picks up more steam, it’ll be hard to see stronger gains in 2026 and 2027.

Another risk to Trex involves competitive pressures like aggressive marketing spend from rivals and potential share shifts at the distributor or contractor level, especially as channel consolidation accelerates through M&A. While Trex hasn’t seen material share loss on the ground (they still hold a #2 position in the market with a 7% share), elevated competitor advertising could erode margins further, which would support a lower EPS run rate and a lower multiple.

Valuation isn't all that attractive

On valuation, analysts are mixed on the outlook with 10 ‘buy’ ratings, 8 ‘hold’ ratings, and 2 ‘sell’ ratings. From the current price to the average price target of $43.55 one year out, analysts see about 1.5% upside from these levels. That likely indicates that they see Trex as being closer to fair value.

Overall, I’d agree with this assessment. At 15.1x EV/EBITDA, this isn’t exactly a cheap stock, especially when we consider that the average multiple against peers is around 12.8x. With continued pressure on EBITDA likely in 2026 and margins above pre-pandemic levels, I don’t see much upside unless we saw a strong boom in the housing market.

Bottom line

Trex has a strong brand and a solid balance sheet, but I think the near-term setup is challenging. There's persistent weakness in repair and remodel demand, limited margin expansion opportunities, and competitive pressures that will constrain earnings growth. In my view, because of those factors, the current valuation already reflects a fair bit of optimism from the market. Without a clear inflection in housing activity or consumer spending, I believe the upside appears limited from today’s levels, making Trex a 'hold.'

data.title: Terrestrial Energy: Compelling Nuclear Play Gaining Momentum For DOE Deals
data.description:
We present our follow-up note on Terrestrial Energy (IMSR) following its business combination. While the share price has declined materially since our initiation of coverage, primarily driven by a shift in investor sentiment around the nuclear renaissance and the SMR theme in particular, we believe the sector’s long-term growth story remains intact, and the investment case for Terrestrial specifically remains compelling. We continue to see an attractive asymmetric return profile in the stock, while acknowledging the highly speculative nature of investing in such an early-stage nuclear reactor technology company with significant regulatory and commercial hurdles to clear. We are also encouraged by the recent agreements with the DOE for a pilot reactor and a pilot molten salt fuel production facility, which we believe further support Terrestrial’s chances of long-term success, while not serving as final validation of the technology. In this note, we briefly comment on the waning momentum of the SMR theme in equity markets, provide our views on the recent DOE agreements, and revise our valuation and investment case to better reflect the current market environment and the most recent developments.

Changing sentiment around nuclear and multiple compression

In our previous note, we outlined the growth story behind SMRs, highlighting the growing need for clean baseload power driven by AI data centers and broader electrification trends, as well as rising policy support for nuclear energy. The nuclear renaissance theme was embraced by equity markets, as reflected in the valuations of nuclear-related stocks in 2025 and in significant equity capital markets activity, including IPOs and SPAC transactions (including Terrestrial Energy completing its reverse merger). This favorable outlook for nuclear and SMRs in particular led to investor enthusiasm and elevated valuations across the sector, with OKLO having reached a market capitalization in excess of $25 billion pre-revenue, serving as a prime example. In addition, some lower-quality nuclear reactor companies, such as Terra Innovatum, achieved market capitalizations in excess of $1 billion despite what we believe are very limited prospects for broad commercial adoption. Valuations have largely corrected over the past couple of months, and we believe there remains further downside risk in several lower-quality names, as well as in the more richly valued OKLO, on which we will publish a dedicated note in the coming days.

That said, we still think that there is a strong case for the long-term growth of SMRs, which we believe has remained intact over the past several months despite a broader “sanity check” in SMR equity valuations. We reiterate our view that Terrestrial is better positioned than most SMR start-up peers (excluding some SMR designs developed by major nuclear corporations) and offers the most attractive risk/reward profile in the SMR start-up universe (excluding NuScale), while acknowledging the highly speculative nature of investing in pre-revenue companies developing novel technologies that remain to be licensed. Terrestrial’s share price declined sharply, bottoming in late December at more than 75% below its peak reached around mid-October 2025. This move was driven primarily by the factors discussed above, as well as profit-taking. The stock has since rebounded in January, supported not only by improving sentiment but also by the announcement of two agreements with the DOE, which are supportive of the company’s prospects.

DOE agreement for Projects TETRA and TEFLA

In early January, Terrestrial announced that it had signed a deal with the Department of Energy for Project TETRA, an initiative to construct and operate a pilot IMSR reactor, allowing the company to transition more efficiently from design to operation under the DOE’s authority within a flexible framework, which we believe may help smooth the IMSR’s path to commercialization. Under the same framework, OKLO also announced an agreement with the DOE to construct and operate a pilot radioisotope production facility.

Separately, this week Terrestrial announced another agreement with the DOE for Project TEFLA, a pilot production facility to demonstrate IMSR fuel salt manufacturing. In a similar fashion to Project TETRA, the development and operation of the fuel salt facility are expected to be facilitated under the DOE framework. Terrestrial’s share price rallied following these announcements. While we do not view these OTAs as representing validation of the technology, and are aware that significant regulatory and commercial hurdles remain (which have been discussed in our previous note), they represent incremental progress and a constructive step forward for the company.

Updated investment case and valuation – fundamentals unchanged

We value Terrestrial using the same methodology as in our previous note, with slight adjustments to better reflect the current environment. We continue to assume that $140mn of EBITDA per plant is achievable and believe that, upon receiving regulatory approvals in the early 2030s, Terrestrial will be able to deploy two plants per year, reaching c. $280mn of EBITDA (simplified figure, which includes pre-construction, construction, component supply, fuel supply, and additional services over the life of the reactor for the purpose of this valuation exercise). However, unlike in our first note, we apply a 20x EBITDA multiple on 2035 EBITDA, implying an enterprise value of $5.6bn, to better reflect the market conditions. Discounting this back to today at a 10% cost of capital results in an enterprise value of $2.2bn today. Assuming a quarterly cash burn of $20mn and a net cash position of $260mn (from PIPE and SPAC proceeds), this implies an equity value of $2.45bn, or 88% upside to the current market cap, and a target share price of around $23.

It is possible to run sensitivities across different scenarios for the company and reach a broad range of valuations depending on the number of reactors deployed, EBITDA per reactor, target EBITDA multiple, and cost of capital. Valuing such an early-stage company is more art than science. Where we see the biggest weakness in our own valuation methodology (which we would like to flag to readers) is the binary nature of the outcome, i.e., whether Terrestrial manages to obtain regulatory approvals or not. Beyond that point, we believe that, provided the economics laid out in the investor materials are somewhat reasonable (and we don’t make any judgment calls on this), the outlook remains attractive. We believe that even if LCOE were c. $100/MWh, or double what is currently presented, the outlook for adoption would still be favorable. However, at materially higher LCOE levels, even in a scenario where regulatory approvals are obtained, higher costs per MWh could begin to negatively affect prospects of commercial adoption.

Where we think Terrestrial truly makes sense, though, is on a relative basis versus other SMR developers or as a pair trade. Oklo has 10x the market capitalization of Terrestrial on what we believe is an inferior business model, being a builder-owner-operator with high capital needs and significant dilution ahead, even if they succeed in getting regulatory approvals and commercial adoption, supply chain constraints such as reliance on unavailable HALEU fuel, and a lot more investor hype.

Our final recommendation is to buy Terrestrial Energy shares, although we believe this should be a rather small allocation in one’s portfolio and in no way a core holding, given the inherent risks discussed above. We believe the risk/reward is attractive at this price, with the potential for a multibagger on one side and a total loss on the other, which we believe is less likely. Given this risk/reward profile, we recommend building a very small speculative position. Alternatively, shorting a basket of higher-valued or lower-quality SMR stocks and going long Terrestrial Energy shares could be an interesting way to play this theme with less beta to SMRs. However, this strategy would still be risky due to the high volatility of the stocks and the significant moves on announcements, which could lead to short squeezes.

Risks

The downside risks have not materially changed from those outlined in our previous note. We believe the key risks include not receiving regulatory approvals, higher-than-expected LCOEs and therefore unattractive economics, declining support for nuclear and SMRs in particular, increased competition, lack of capital, dilution, etc.

Conclusion

We recommend buying Terrestrial Energy shares, given their interesting upside, particularly on a relative basis, while acknowledging the highly speculative nature of the investment at this stage, and suggest that this remain a small position in the portfolio rather than a core holding.

data.title: Volatility Fades As Markets Refocus On Fundamentals
data.description:
Markets rebound as tariff fears ease

It was a volatile week in financial markets, largely driven by geopolitical developments. Last weekend, the U.S. administration proposed new tariffs on several European countries linked to tensions around Greenland. However, following discussions at the World Economic Forum in Davos, those tariffs were ultimately suspended, helping to calm investor nerves.

Markets reacted swiftly to the shifting headlines. At its low point earlier in the week, the S&P 500 was down nearly 2% before rebounding sharply and ending the week close to all-time highs. In our view, the speed of the recovery underscores that the underlying fundamentals—both for the global business cycle and corporate earnings—remain sound.

Signs of stability in U.S. labor market

Developments related to the U.S. business cycle were also in focus. First, U.S. real GDP (gross domestic product) growth for the third quarter was revised higher to an annualized pace of 4.4%, a notably strong number by historical standards.

More important, however, were fresh signals from the labor market. The U.S. has been operating in a “low-hire, low-fire” environment for several months, but this week both initial and continuing jobless claims surprised to the downside. This suggests labor market conditions may be stabilizing, which would be a positive sign for the broader economy.

We see these trends as consistent with our expectation of a healthy U.S. economy through 2026, with the potential for growth to reaccelerate over the coming quarters.

Japanese bond yields in focus

Japan has also remained in the spotlight. Expectations of additional fiscal stimulus have pushed long-term Japanese government bond yields higher, with yields reaching their highest levels since the late 1990s earlier this week.

By Thursday’s close, however, the situation appeared to stabilize after comments from Japan’s finance minister saying the government is taking active steps to stabilize the bond market. That reassurance helped to put a floor under sentiment—not only in Japan but across global fixed-income markets more broadly.

data.title: Data Update 2 For 2026: Equities Get Tested And Pass Again
data.description:
It was a disquieting year, as political and economic news stories shook the foundations of the post-war economic order, built around global trade and the US dollar. In fact, if you had just read the news all through the year and were shielded from financial markets and then asked what stocks did during the year, you would have guessed, based on the news, that they had a bad year. You would have been wrong, though, as equity markets proved resilient (yet again) and delivered another solid year of returns for investors. In this post, I will focus on US equities, starting with the indices and then deconstructing the data to see the differences in the cross-section. As has been my practice for the last few years, I will also use this post to update the equity risk premium for the S&P 500, my composite indicator for whether the market is richly priced or not, and estimate a value for the index with a "reasonable" equity risk premium.

Back from the Brink: US Equities in 2025

At the start of 2025, the consensus view was that stocks were primed to do well, helped by what investors perceived would be a business-friendly administration and a Federal Reserve ready to cut rates. In keeping with Robert Burns' phrase that the best-laid plans of mice and men go awry, the year did not measure up to those expectations, at least in terms of policy and rate changes, but stocks still managed to find a way through. Let's start with a look at the S&P 500 and the NASDAQ, day-to-day through the year:

The first few weeks of 2025 saw a continuation of the momentum built up after the 2024 elections, and stock prices continued upwards, but February and March saw a drawdown in stock prices as talk of tariffs and trade wars heated up before culminating in a dramatic sell-off in early April, after Liberation Day, when breadth and magnitude of the tariffs blindsided markets. The sell-off was brutal and short, and stocks hit their low point for the year on April 11, 2025. Over the next few months, stocks mounted a comeback before leveling off at the end of September and coasting for the rest of the year. Early in the year, the S&P 500 held its value better than the NASDAQ, generating talk of a long-awaited tech sell-off, but as stocks recovered in the subsequent months, the NASDAQ ended up moving ahead of the S&P 500.

Across the entire year, the S&P 500 rose from 5881.6 to 6845.5, delivering price appreciation of 16.39% for the year. The dividends on the companies in the index for the year, based upon dividends in the first three quarters of 2025 and estimates for dividends in the last quarter, amounted to an added yield of 1.34%.

The S&P 500's return in 2025 of 17.72% was a solid year, but to provide perspective on how it measures up to history, I looked at annual returns from US stocks from 1928 to 2025 and computed distributional statistics:

While 2025's annual returns put it right in the middle of the distribution, close to the median, and ranked 45th of the 98 years of US equity returns from 1928 to 2026, it represented a third consecutive year when the annual stock return exceeded the median returns, the longest streak since the mid-1990s. US equities between 2023 and 2025, a period when many market timers were suggesting not just caution but staying out of the market, returned 85.32% to investors.

Deconstructing US Stock Price Performance

While stocks had a good year overall, the spoils were divided unequally, as is often the case, across industries and sectors. To take a closer look at where the best and worst performance was in 2025, I started by looking at a breakdown by sector, where I computed the returns based on the change in aggregate market capitalization in 2025:

I have tracked the performance of each sector by quarter and across the year, and measured the returns. The best-performing sector in percentage returns was communication services (which includes Alphabet and Meta), up 30.63% for the year, followed by technology, which continued its sustained run of success by delivering 23.65% as an annual return. On a dollar value basis, it was not close with technology companies posting an increase of $4.17 trillion in market cap during the year. The worst-performing sectors were consumer staples and real estate, where the returns were about 2% for the year.

The problem with sector categorizations is that they are overly broad and include very diverse industry groupings, and to overcome that problem, I looked at returns by industry, with a breakdown into 95 industry groups. While you can find the full list at the end of this post, I ranked the industry returns in 2025, from best to worst, and extracted the ten best and worst performing industry groups:

The surge in gold and silver prices in 2025 carried precious metals companies to the top of the list, with a return of 169.2% for the year, and other energy and mining companies also made the best performer list, with a scattering of technology standouts. The worst-performing businesses were primarily old economy, with chemicals, consumer product companies and food processing, all struggling during the year.

One of the major changes that we have seen in cross-sectional differences in the twenty-first century has been the fading or even disappearance of two well documented phenomena from the twentieth century, the first being the small cap premium, where small market cap companies delivered much higher risk-adjusted returns than large market cap companies, and the value premium, where low price-to-book stocks beat high price-to-book stocks in the return game. I focused on how these categorizations behaved in 2025, and we did see small cap stocks and low price-to-book stocks return, at least in part, to favor:

If you are a small cap or a value investor, though, I would not be celebrating the return on these premia, but I do think that we will start to see a return to balance, where the groupings will trade off winning in some years for losing in others.

As a final assessment, I did look at the seven stocks that have not only carried the market for the last few years, the Mag Seven, but have also been the source of much hand-wringing about how markets are becoming top-heavy and concentrated. I started by looking at the individual companies and how they performed in 2025:

While the Mag Seven saw their collective market capitalization increase by 22.36%, Apple (AAPL) and Amazon (AMZN) lagged with single-digit increases, and Nvidia (NVDA) (up 37.8%) and Alphabet (GOOGL) (up 62.7%) for the year. Increasingly, the Mag Seven are diverging in their price paths, and that should be expected since they operate in very different businesses and have very different management running them. To examine how much the Mag Seven have carried the market, I tracked the market cap of the Mag Seven against the rest of US equity (close to 6000 companies) from 2014 through the four quarters of 2025.

The aggregate market cap of the Mag Seven has increased from 11% of the US equity market (composed of close to 6000 stocks) in 2014 to 30.89% of the market at the end of 2025, with the $3.9 billion in market cap added in 2025 accounting for 39.3% of the overall increase in market capitalization of all US equities during the year. While this Mag Seven party will undoubtedly end at some point, it did not happen in 2025.

US Equities: Too high, too low, or just right?

This post, at least so far, has been a postmortem of the year that was, but investing is always about the future, and the question that we all face as investors is where stocks will go this year. In my unscientific assessment of stock market opinion from experts and market timers, there seems to be a decided tilt towards bearishness at the start of 2026 for a variety of reasons. There are some who note that, having had three good years in a row, stocks will take a breather. Others point to history and note that stocks generally don't do well in the second years of presidential terms. The most common metric that bearish investors point to, though, is the PE ratio for stocks at the start of 2026, is pushing towards historic highs, as can be seen in the graph below, where I look at three variants on the PE ratio - a trailing PE, where I divide the index by earnings in the most recent 12 months; a normalized PE, where I divide the index by the average earnings over the last ten years; and a Shiller PE, where I average inflation-adjusted earnings over the last ten years:

Using every PE ratio measure, it is undeniable that the PE ratio for the S&P 500, at the start of 2026, is much higher than it has been at any extended period in history, perhaps with the exception of the late 1990s. While this may sound like a slam-dunk argument for US stocks being overpriced, it is worth remembering that this indicator would have suggested staying out of US equities for much of the last decade. The problem with the PE pricing metric is that it is noisy and an unreliable indicator, and before you use it to build a case that equity investors in the US have become irrational, you may want to consider reasons why US stocks have been able to fight the gravitational forces of mean reversion.

1. Robust Earnings Growth & Earnings Resilience: In this century, US stocks have increased more than fourfold, with the S&P 500 rising from 1320.28 at the end of 2000 to 6845.5 at the end of 2025. But it is also worth noting that US companies have also had a solid run in earnings, with earnings increasing about 356% during that same time period.

It is also notable that not only did earnings register strong growth over this period, but there were also only three years in this century when earnings declined - 2001 (dot-com bust), 2009 (2008 crisis) and 2020 (Covid). US companies have become more resilient in terms of delivering earnings through recessions and other crises, pointing to perhaps less risk in equities. I will return in a later post to examine why that may be, with some of the answers rooted in changes in US equity market composition and some in management behavior.

2. Healthy cash returns: In conjunction with delivering earnings growth, US companies have also been returning large amounts of cash to their shareholders, albeit more in buybacks than in conventional dividends. In 2025, the companies in the S&P 500 alone returned more than a trillion dollars in cash flows in buybacks, and in the graph below, I look at how the augmented cash yield (composed of dividends and buybacks) has largely sustained the market:

While the dividend payout ratio, computed using only dividends, has been on a downward trend all through this century, adding buybacks to dividends and computing a cash yield ratio yields values that are comparable to what dividend yields used to be before the buyback era.

In sum, you can see why both bulls and bears retreat to their favored arguments, and there is no obvious tiebreaker. The level of stock prices (PE ratios) should be a concern, but you cannot dismiss the benefits of growing and resilient earnings and substantial cash return. To break the tie, in a very self-serving way, I will revert to my favored metric for the US equity market, the implied equity risk premium, which, in addition to looking at stock price levels, the growth in earnings, and the cash return, also brings in the level of rates. The implied equity risk premium, as I compute it, is based upon the index level and the expected cash flows (from dividends and buybacks, augmented by earnings growth) and, very simply, is an internal rate of return for stocks. Netting out the risk-free rate yields an equity risk premium. The table below contains the computation of the implied ERP at the start of 2026:

Given the index level on January 1, 2026, of 6845.5, and the expected cash flows that I computed on that date (using the dividends and buybacks in the trailing 12 months as my starting point and growing them at the same rate as earnings), I obtain an expected return on stocks of 8.41%. Subtracting out the US T. Bond rate (dollar risk-free rate) of 4.18% (3.95%) on that day yields an equity risk premium of 4.23% (4.46%) for the US. I want to emphasize again that this estimate is entirely a market-driven number and is model agnostic.

If you are wondering how estimating these numbers lets you make a judgment on whether US stocks are overpriced, all you need to do is reframe the equity risk premium by asking whether the current ERP is, in your view, too high, too low, or just right.

  • If you believe that the market is pricing in too low an ERP, given the risks that are on the horizon, you are contending the stocks are overpriced.
  • If your view is that the current ERP is too high, that is equivalent to arguing that stocks today are underpriced.
  • If you are not a market timer, you are, in effect, arguing that the current ERP is, in fact, the right ERP for the market.

To illustrate this point, I have estimated the value of the index at equity risk premiums ranging from 2% to 6%:

With a 2% equity risk premium, you get an astounding value of 14834 for the S&P 500, which would make the index undervalued by 53%. At the other end of the spectrum, with a 6% equity risk premium, the index should trade at 4790, translating into an overvaluation of 43%. So, is the ERP of 4.23% (I will revert to this number, since my historical numbers did use the US Treasury bond rate as the risk-free rate) at the start of 2026 a high, low, or just-right number? Rather than make that judgment for you, I have computed the implied ERP for the S&P 500 going back to 1960:

There is something in this graph that almost every investor group can take comfort in. If you are market neutral, you will take comfort from the fact that the current ERP is almost exactly equal to the average for the 1960-2025 period. If you are bearish, you will point to the fact that the ERP now is lower than it has been in the post-2008 period, backing up your case that an adjustment is overdue. I am leery of the bubble word, especially used in the context of this market, since, unlike the end of 1999, when the ERP got as low as 2.05%, the current ERP is more in the middle of the historic range.

The Bottom Line

US equities had a good year in 2025, and there are signs of excess in some parts of the market, especially related to AI. That said, the capacity of US companies to continue to deliver earnings and return cash flows even in the face of a tsunami of bad news continues to sustain the market. I am, at my core, a non-market timer, but I have held back on putting idle cash back into US equities in the last year, preferring to keep that cash in treasury bills. It is entirely possible that the market will continue to prove the naysayers wrong and post another strong year, but much as it may pain equity investors, the healthiest development for the market would be for it to deliver a return roughly equal to its expected return (8-9%) and clean up on pricing overreach along the way. For the bears, this may also be the year when the bad news stories of last year, including tariffs and political whiplash, will finally start to hit the bottom line, reducing aggregate earnings and cash flows, but waiting on the sidelines for this to happen has not been a good strategy for the last decade.

data.title: Lithium Junior Miners News For The Month Of January 2026
data.description:
Welcome to the January 2026 edition of the 'junior' lithium miner news. We have categorized those lithium miners that are not in commercial production as the juniors.

Note: Investors are reminded that many of the lithium juniors will most likely not be needed until the late 2020s (& 2030s) to supply the potentially booming electric vehicle [EV] and battery energy stationary storage [BESS] markets. This means investing in these companies requires a higher risk tolerance and a longer time frame.

The past month, China lithium carbonate spot prices and spodumene prices surged significantly higher.

Lithium price news

Metal.com reported a lithium spodumene concentrate Index (Li2O 5.5%-6.2%, excluding tax/insurance/freight) spot price of USD 2,130, as of January 22, 2026, surging higher the past month.

The China lithium carbonate spot price surged over 60% higher last month, now at CNY 164,500.

China lithium carbonate spot price 5 year chart - CNY 164,500 (~USD 23,621) (source)

Katusa Research is forecasting a small lithium deficit to begin in 2026 (as of January 2026) (source)

RMIS forecasts lithium deficits to start after 2028, may grow very large without major new investments (as of May 2025) (source)

GGII, a Shenzhen-based consultancy, forecasts a balanced lithium market this year, a 10,000t LCE deficit in 2026, and a 60,000t deficit in 2027 (as of mid 2025) (source)

Lithium market news

For a summary of the latest lithium market news and the 'major' lithium company's news, investors can read: "Lithium Miners News For The Month Of January 2026" article. Highlights include:

  • The China lithium carbonate spot price surged over 60% higher last month.
  • Katusa Research is forecasting a small lithium deficit to begin in 2026.
  • CATL's key lithium mine is expected to resume production around Chinese New Year.
  • CATL confirms 2026 large-scale sodium-ion battery deployment in multiple sectors.
  • Fastmarkets: Lithium prices surge amid supply squeeze and speculative frenzy.
  • Trading Economics: Lithium carbonate futures surged past CNY 150,000/t in January. Chinese authorities lowered export rebates for battery producers from April, driving manufacturers to front-run lithium orders. Authorities stated 27 mining permits were canceled in the lithium hub of Jiangxi.
  • CATL signs a $17.2B LFP cathode material order with Ronbay over 5 years.
  • Argentina sets out 10-year plan for lithium growth. Possible doubling of national production capacity by the early 2030s if priority projects proceed as planned.
  • Lithium swings from glut to scarcity on Asia demand, Traxys says. Surging BESS shipments are forecast to strongly boost lithium demand.
  • China EV battery installations rise 40.4% to 769.7 GWh in 2025, LFP share at 81.2%. China's combined production of power and energy storage batteries in December reached 201.7 GWh, up 62.1% YoY.
  • US lawmakers introduce bill to create $2.5 billion critical-minerals stockpile.

Junior lithium miners company news

LibertyStream Infrastructure Partners Inc. [TSXV:LIB]

On January 6, LibertyStream announced:

LibertyStream achieves consistent, spec-compliant lithium carbonate production from automated refining unit in the Texas Permian Basin... Following customer qualification, LibertyStream plans to advance to bulk sampling and offtake discussions to support scale-up in 2026, targeting commercial volumes beginning in 2027.

On January 14, LibertyStream announced:

Special meeting of shareholders to approve re-domiciliation to Texas as part of its strategic pathway toward a future U.S. exchange listing.

On January 19, LibertyStream announced: "Upsize of non-brokered LIFE offering to up to $12,500,000."

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • H1, 2026 - Start of small scale commercial production from Permian Basin oilfield brine.
  • 2027 - Targeting commercial volumes beginning in 2027.

Galan Lithium [ASX:GLN] (GLNLF)

Galan is developing their flagship Hombre Muerto West ("HMW") Lithium Project located on the west side edge of the high grade, low impurity Hombre Muerto Salar in Argentina.

On January 20, Galan Lithium announced:

Quarterly Activities and Cashflow Report.

  • Phase 1 construction at Hombre Muerto West (HMW) on track for first production in H1 2026, well timed to take advantage of a lithium market recovery.
  • Strategic expansion of Greenbushes South tenure.
  • Early completion of the second tranche of the Clean Elements leading to a strong financial position with cash of $15M. (as at 31 December2025), no debt and an undrawn US$ 6 M. prepayment facility.

The Phase 1 nano-filtration plant was assembled and tested in Sydney, confirming performance in accordance with design parameters. Following testing, the plant was containerised and dispatched for shipment to site, with delivery expected in early 2026 and commissioning to follow.

Upcoming catalysts include:

Galan Lithium HMW site shows Phase 1 construction is well advanced (source)

Atlas Lithium Corp. (ATLX)
